data.title: Indonesian ex-minister rebuffs claims of China bias amid ‘state within a state’ airport row
data.description:
A former Indonesian minister has dismissed claims of giving preferential treatment to Chinese firms and wooing investors with policies undermining the country’s sovereignty, amid furore over an airport inside a major industrial cluster that risks becoming a “state within a state”.
Luhut Pandjaitan, a key architect of ex-president Joko Widodo’s mineral downstreaming strategy, said China’s technological prowess and deep pockets made it an ideal partner for propelling the local economy as others like the US were reluctant to finance projects and share the expertise with Jakarta in the past.
“China was the only one ready to step in [at the time],” the former coordinating maritime affairs and investment minister said in a statement on Monday.
“We don’t side with China or the United States, we side with Indonesia,” he said, crediting downstreaming for the country’s gross domestic product which was expected to grow between 4.9 per cent and 5.7 per cent next year.
Downstreaming refers to processing critical minerals domestically.
The US did not have the tech capability to engage in downstreaming and was “far behind China”, Luhut said, citing a discussion with Tesla chief Elon Musk, The Jakarta Post reported.
He previously met the billionaire in Texas in 2022 to convince him to invest in the electric vehicle battery industry in Indonesia, home to some of the world’s largest deposits of copper, nickel and tin.

Luhut maintained that all investments approved by the Widodo administration were subjected to an “integrated and transparent process” but acknowledged that some concessions needed to be offered when courting megaprojects.
Bilateral trade between the two countries in 2024 reached US$135 billion, making China Indonesia’s largest trading partner.
If investors pledged US$20 billion, it was “reasonable” for them to request certain facilities like airports, though they should comply with local rules, he said.
His response came after an airport inside the Indonesia Morowali Industrial Park in Central Sulawesi province was accused of operating international flights without authorisation.
Luhut called on everyone to work towards Indonesia’s development and stop turning the 2,000-hectare (4,942-acre) industrial hub, dotted with Chinese companies, into a political football.
Last week, Defence Minister Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin inspected the airport and said the alleged operation of international routes without adequate supervision from regulators could create a “state within a state” inside the industrial corridor.
“An airport operating without the presence of state authorities is an anomaly in the republic of Indonesia, and regulations must be enforced,” Sjafrie told local media.
Deputy Transport Minister Suntana refuted Sjafrie’s assertion, saying the airport “operates with all required permits” and had customs and police officers.
The airport received international status from Suntana’s ministry in August before it was cancelled two months later.

Chappy Hakim, a former air force chief of staff, said Morowali had “become a political stage shaped by power struggles among the country’s elites”.
The war of words between the two ministers was “a reflection of competing priorities: sovereignty and defence on one side, and administrative legality and investment certainty on the other”, he told The Jakarta Post.
In June, the environment ministry promised to undertake a sweeping audit of Morowali after uncovering violations that included excessive air pollution and an unlicensed waste processing facility.
The industrial zone, jointly owned by China’s Tsingshan Holding Group and Indonesia’s Bintang Delapan Group, has also been plagued by a string of fatal accidents.
A smelter explosion killed 13 Indonesian and eight Chinese workers at the complex in 2023, and three people died when a landslide struck a waste storage unit in March.

data.title: Prada has bought Versace for $1.4 billion. Can it make its Milan rival ‘interesting again’?
data.description:
The Prada Group closed the purchase of Milan fashion rival Versace in a US$1.4 billion deal that puts the fashion house known for its sexy silhouettes under the same roof as Prada’s “ugly chic” aesthetic and Miu Miu’s youth-driven appeal.
The highly anticipated deal is expected to relaunch Versace’s fortunes following a middling post-pandemic performance as part of the American luxury group Capri.
Prada said in a one-line statement that the acquisition had been completed after receiving all regulatory clearances. Capri, which owns Michael Kors and Jimmy Choo, said the money would be used to pay down debt.
Donatella Versace welcomed the deal in an Instagram post, which also marked the birthday of the brand’s late founder, her brother, Gianni Versace.
“Today is your day and the day Versace joins the Prada family,” she wrote in a post that also featured a 1996 photo of Gianni Versace with Miuccia Prada. “I am thinking of the smile you would have had on your face.”

Prada heir Lorenzo Bertelli is set to steer Versace’s next phase as executive chairman, in addition to his roles as group marketing director and sustainability chief.
The son of co-creative director Miuccia Prada and long-time Prada Group chairman Patrizio Bertelli has said he does not expect to make any swift executive changes at Versace. He also noted that the company, which is among the top 10 most recognised brands in the world, has long been underperforming in the market.
Prada has underlined that the 47-year-old Versace brand offered “significant untapped growth potential”.
The appeal of the deal is that it combines “the minimalist Prada [with] a maximalist Versace”, said Luca Solca, managing director for the luxury sector at the Sanford C. Bernstein research firm, meaning that the brands do not compete for the same customers.
Versace is “long past its heyday”, Solca said.
“The challenge and the opportunity is to make it relevant again. They are going to have to invent something which is going to make the brand attractive, desirable and interesting again.”

Versace has already begun a creative relaunch under a new designer, Dario Vitale, who previewed his first collection during Milan Fashion Week in September. He was previously head of design at Miu Miu, but his move to Versace was unrelated to the Prada deal, executives have said.
The runway show received mixed reviews, but the collection itself – a colourful, revealing riff on the 1980s – got good feedback from buyers.
“I think that this seems to be a promising first step,” Solca said.
Capri paid US$2 billion for Versace in 2018, but had been struggling to position the brand’s bold profile in the recent era of “quiet luxury”.
Capri chairman John D. Idol said in a statement that “Prada is the ideal partner to guide this celebrated luxury house into its next era of growth”.
The Prada Group has already begun preparations to incorporate crosstown rival Versace into its Italian manufacturing system, a point of pride for the group.
“Making a bag for one brand or another, the know-how is the same,” Bertelli told reporters last week at the group’s Scandicci leather goods factory, which already makes bags for the Prada and Miu Miu brands and will soon add Versace.

Artisans stitched handles onto leather bags and cut leather with laser machines inside the leather goods factory, where trainees were learning the trade as part of Prada’s 25-year-old academy.
It has trained some 570 new artisans in an in-house training programme in the Tuscany, Marche, Veneto and Umbria regions.
In 2024, Prada hired 70 per cent of the 120 artisans who trained in the academy. The number of trainees rose by 28 per cent to 152 this year.
The Prada Group has invested €60 million (US$70 million) in its supply chain this year, including a new leather goods factory near Siena, a new knitwear factory near Perugia, as well as increasing production at its Church’s footwear factory in Britain and expanding another Tuscan factory. That is on top of €200 million in investments from 2019 to 2024.

data.title: Huawei launches app for AirDrop-style file transfers between HarmonyOS and Apple devices
data.description:
Chinese telecoms giant Huawei Technologies has launched a new app to facilitate seamless data exchange between its proprietary operating system HarmonyOS and Apple’s iOS ecosystem.
The “HarmonyOS Interconnect” app was recently made available on the App Store for MacOS devices, days after the release of the version for the iPhone and iPad. Once installed on an Apple device, it allows AirDrop-style transfers of files, images, videos and contact information between a device running on the latest HarmonyOS 6.
The move was promised by Huawei in late October when launching the HarmonyOS 6. At the time it said the new system would enable direct file transfers between Huawei and Apple devices for the first time.
HarmonyOS 6, which was initially made available to developers in June, is expected to be found in more than 90 Huawei devices after a software update by year end. The newly launched Mate 80 and foldable Mate X7 smartphones, announced last week, already run on the system.
Huawei launched HarmonyOS as an Android alternative for the Chinese market in August 2019, just months after the US government added the company to a trade blacklist that blocked it from buying US-origin technologies without Washington’s approval.

In a 2024 update, HarmonyOS added a “tap-to-transfer” feature, allowing users to easily share content by physically touching one device on the other. It was not clear if the new app would also facilitate such a feature.
Huawei did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Wednesday.
With its aggressive marketing efforts, HarmonyOS has maintained a lead over Apple’s iOS for the seventh consecutive quarter in the mainland Chinese market. It accounted for 18 per cent of China’s smartphone operating system market in the third quarter, compared with 14 per cent for iOS. That made the Huawei system the second largest in China behind Android, which held 69 per cent of the market, according to Counterpoint Research.
Globally, HarmonyOS was installed in 4 per cent of smartphones sold in the most recent quarter, trailing far behind Android and iOS, with 79 per cent and 17 per cent, respectively, according to the research firm.

data.title: Filipino helper on her second day of work when Tai Po fire hit, escapes with baby
data.description:
Filipino domestic helper Rhodora Alcaraz arrived in Hong Kong on Tuesday last week and went straight to work at her employer’s flat in Tai Po, finding herself battling flames a day later to make a miraculous escape with a three-month-old baby in her arms.
The 27-year-old is now in an intensive care unit, unable to speak above a whisper, after suffering injuries, including to her throat, which made her feel like she had “poison pouring down her throat”.
Last week, she arrived from her Central Luzon hometown in the Philippines, full of plans to save up enough to help her younger teenage brother finish college.
Little did she know her second day at work would also be the day of the biggest fire in Hong Kong in decades, and she would be swept up in it, one of the 79 people injured in the deadly blaze that claimed at least 159 lives.
She was at the flat in Wang Fuk Court with the baby and her female employer that afternoon while the husband was at work. The fire broke out at 2.51pm, quickly escalating in less than four hours.
From accounts of her relatives in Hong Kong and the Philippines, Alcaraz had the presence of mind to care for her little ward.
After being in the building for three hours, Alcaraz and her female employer managed to make a miraculous escape, with the helper swaddling the baby in a wet blanket.
Relatives said she knew what to do because she herself is a mother of a five-year-old girl.

Alcaraz was unconscious when she was rushed away by rescuers and listed as critically injured at United Christian Hospital’s intensive care unit.
She is now stable, but struggles to talk, telling those visiting her how the smoke from the fire was like poison going down her throat.
Unable to get up in bed, she still cannot eat solid food and is being fed a liquid diet intravenously, along with much-needed medication.
“We were not able to really converse. She whispers if she can,” said Cynthia Abdon-Tellez, general manager of the Mission for Migrant Workers (MFMW).
“I told her, ‘you’re so brave’, and then she pointed to the ceiling, wordlessly trying to say that ‘God is with me’. I told her you saved a life, not only yours,” Abdon-Tellez said.
She said Alcaraz had wrapped the baby in a damp blanket and knew what to do because she herself was a mother.
Alcaraz has not been visited by her employers, but she suspected her female employer might still be in a critical condition in another hospital.
The baby was discharged after being admitted, apparently largely unscathed.
Abdon-Tellez was able to visit Alcaraz, who had not known she had become a social media celebrity and hailed a hero among Filipino workers in Hong Kong and by her own country, at the hospital after the brave helper’s cousin, Jen, had sought help from the MFMW on Sunday. The pair went to the hospital immediately.
Eman Villanueva, spokesman for the Asian Migrants’ Coordinating Body and a board member of MFMW, has also visited Alcaraz.
Villanueva said he was “friends” on social media with the woman’s younger brother, Elmo, who lives in the family’s hometown in San Pedro.
The brother told the Post he was the reason his older sibling was working as a domestic helper in Hong Kong.




His sister went to work in Qatar for several years just so she could help her younger siblings afford schooling. She then returned to the Philippines and stayed there for 1½ years.
“Then she told me she would apply for a helper’s job in Hong Kong as she had so much experience,” he said.
“She worked in a small store, a company and as a maid in San Pablo, just so she could earn and support us.”
Still, money was tight. Elmo is the only one in the family to attend college – he wants to be a teacher after he obtains a bachelor’s degree in physical education. Their youngest sibling is still in high school.
“And now this fire in Hong Kong happened. It is very traumatic for our family. We don’t want her to go abroad again because of it,” Elmo Alcaraz said.
He remembers her sister’s last words to him that fateful day she flew to Hong Kong, only to meet with tragedy.
“She told me, ‘In Hong Kong, if my lot turns out better, I will support your studies. So please always pray that Hong Kong will be good for me’,” he said. “We are all shocked by what happened, but thank God that my sister is alive.”

data.title: Japan’s Sanae Takaichi reaffirms Taiwan is a part of China
data.description:
Japan’s position on Taiwan remains unchanged, Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said on Wednesday, referring to a 1972 commitment that led to the normalisation of ties between Beijing and Tokyo.
“The Japanese government’s basic position regarding Taiwan remains as stated in the 1972 Japan-China joint statement, and there has been no change to this position,” Takaichi told the Japanese parliament.
According to the 1972 statement, “the government of the People’s Republic of China reiterates that Taiwan is an inalienable part of the territory of the People’s Republic of China” and the Japanese government “fully understands and respects this stand”.
Takaichi’s comments come nearly a month after she told the parliament that an attack on Taiwan by the PLA could constitute a “survival-threatening situation” – one that could allow Tokyo to engage in military action.
It was the most explicit statement by a sitting Japanese prime minister on how Tokyo might respond to a Taiwan contingency and marked a departure from the country’s long-held strategic ambiguity over the issue – a position the US has also maintained, and plummet bilateral relations to a record low.
More to follow...

data.title: China trains industry AI tool using ‘world’s largest’ port operation data set
data.description:
China is training an artificial intelligence tool to support the country’s port industry using what the National Development and Reform Commission said was the “world’s largest” port operation data set.
The AI application draws on data covering “the most comprehensive range of port business scenarios”, according to Li Chao, deputy director of the NDRC’s policy research office.
“It will provide strong support for technological research and development as well as verification in areas such as port operations and autonomous driving,” Li told reporters in Beijing on Thursday.
“It has gathered [data from] hundreds of AI technology suppliers and port sector users, significantly lowering the threshold for AI application development in the port industry,” she added.
China was home to six of the world’s top 10 container ports in terms of volume in 2024, according to trade association the World Shipping Council.
Those ports are Shanghai, Ningbo-Zhoushan, Shenzhen, Qingdao, Guangzhou and Tianjin.


Annual container throughput at the Ningbo-Zhoushan Port exceeded 40 million 20-foot equivalent units – the standard measure for a freight container – for the first time on Tuesday, state news agency Xinhua reported.
The port, located in the eastern province of Zhejiang, connects to more than 600 ports across more than 200 countries and regions.
It is also one of the world’s top ports in terms of large and ultra-large deepwater berth capacity.
Outside the country, Chinese state-owned enterprises have invested in 129 ports across dozens of countries, mostly in the Global South, according to US-based think tank the Council on Foreign Relations.
However, ownership of overseas ports has become a focal point of geopolitical tension between China and several countries, including the United States, over key ports near the Panama Canal owned by Hong Kong-based conglomerate CK Hutchison Holdings and China’s stake in the Greek port of Piraeus.
China’s relationship with Australia has also been strained over the Port of Darwin, which is under a 99-year lease to the Chinese firm Landbridge Group. The port is a strategic asset in Australia’s north and is home to a base for thousands of US marines. The Australian government has pledged to end Chinese ownership of the port but has not given a time frame to do so.
China is pushing to have AI technologies adopted across various sectors, including manufacturing, medical care and transport.
Three major ports – Hubei, Ningbo-Zhoushan and Guangzhou – have already implemented or were planning to adopt the home-grown AI tool DeepSeek to improve efficiency and service quality.
The Ningbo-Zhoushan Port, for example, has incorporated the DeepSeek model into its intelligent gate and customer service system.
The new gate system can recognise more than 80 per cent of containers’ unique seal numbers, cutting the time needed for manual checks. The number of staff required to conduct container inspections has been reduced from four workers to one, according to the port’s owner.
The DeepSeek-powered gate system can also reduce fault alert response times to less than 30 minutes. The technology is used across several terminals processing 20,000 20-foot equivalent units a day.
According to the Hubei Port Group, adopting domestic AI technologies has helped to ensure data security across port operations, facility layout and cargo information.

data.title: Rivals push insurance reforms, easier fire claims in Hong Kong poll race
data.description:
Hong Kong’s insurers should make it easier for residents affected by the deadly Tai Po fire to file claims and consider incorporating fire safety inspection results into underwriting, according to sector representatives contesting a seat in the legislature.
Insurance sector hopefuls Chan Pui-leung and Simon Lam Yat-tung made the appeals on Wednesday during a government-led forum, the first held since last week’s blaze at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, which claimed at least 159 lives.
Chan, the general manager of state-owned China Taiping Insurance (Hong Kong), urged insurers to set up more green channels and streamline procedures to help affected residents claim funds and rebuild their lives after the disaster.
His company is the insurer responsible for the housing estate’s property damage and third-party liability related to its renovation.
It had processed its first batch of nine home insurance claims on Monday, paying out a total of HK$5.37 million (US$689,899) to victims.
Chan, an incumbent Election Committee lawmaker who decided to run in the insurance sector this time, also proposed that the government establish a central mechanism to prevent safety breaches.
He said the current regulatory framework involved multiple departments that lacked adequate communication.
Lam, the co-chief executive officer of China Pacific Life Insurance (Hong Kong) and Chan’s rival, called on the insurance sector to launch a fundraising drive to support the reconstruction of the fire-ravaged estate.
“I also propose incorporating fire safety inspection results into insurance underwriting requirements, to ensure property owners invest in improving building safety and help restore public confidence,” he said.
The government should also provide a clear timetable in reforming its policies in light of the tragedy and allow the public to monitor the progress, he added.
Discussions on post-fire recovery efforts were the major focus of Legislative Council election forums, as the city worked to pick up the pieces after its deadliest blaze in seven decades.
The forums resumed on Wednesday with a minute’s silence in memory of the blaze victims. The cheering session, one of the four segments in past forums, was cancelled.
Two of the three mandatory questions focused on post-fire recovery.
Candidates were asked to outline their sector’s role in tackling similar disasters and how they would support Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s comprehensive policy review and rebuilding efforts.
The city leader on Monday ordered the establishment of a judge-led independent review committee to find out the truth behind the blaze as he pledged to initiate “systemic reforms”.
Lee also decided to proceed with the election as scheduled on Sunday, describing the newly elected lawmakers as key partners in driving these reforms.

An earlier forum in the morning featured representatives from the sector comprising Hong Kong deputies to the National People’s Congress, city members of the National Committee of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference, and representatives of relevant national organisations.
Incumbent lawmaker Chan Yung cautioned the public against anti-China and anti-Hong Kong forces that “do not wish Hong Kong well” and sought to sow chaos as the city worked to support affected residents.
As of Wednesday, three people were arrested on suspicion of seditious intent under the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, including a man who was reportedly a founder of a concern group over the fire.
He had launched a petition urging the government to meet four demands, one of which called for the establishment of a commission of inquiry.
Chan’s opponent Lai Tat-shing proposed the adoption of advanced technology to enhance future relief and rescue work, and the establishment of a dedicated committee to oversee the rebuilding efforts.
Two other forums to be held on Wednesday are for catering and education constituencies.
Among the 161 election candidates, 51 are competing in the geographical constituencies for 20 seats, while 60 are standing in the 28 functional constituencies for 30 seats.
The remaining 50 candidates will contest the 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency.
Residents vote for their representatives for the geographical constituencies, while lawmakers in the functional constituencies and the Election Committee constituency are elected by a small circle of voters in their respective sectors.

data.title: Beijing testing centre warns of sham construction safety reports in Hong Kong
data.description:
A testing centre in Beijing has questioned the veracity of a scaffolding net safety report for a renovation site in Hong Kong, and warned of fraudulent documents circulating in the market that falsely use the lab’s name.
Early investigations by city authorities showed the nets used for renovations at Wang Fuk Court – the site of last week’s deadly fire that killed 159 – did not meet fire safety standards.
But the National Quality Inspection and Testing Center for Labour Protective Equipment in Beijing issued a statement on Wednesday warning that fraudulent reports with safety testing results had recently been circulated.
The reports claimed to come from the centre, but used its old name – the National Quality Supervision and Inspection Center for Labour Protective Equipment (Beijing) – before “Supervision and Inspection” was replaced with “Inspection and Testing”.
“Our centre officially changed its name [ …] starting from August 30, 2021,” the statement said.
“All reports issued after this date use the new name. Any report issued after [ …] that still uses the original name [ …] is a fraudulent report.”
The statement appeared to refer to a safety report on scaffolding nets at Hong Kong’s Fung Wah Estate in Chai Wan, which is currently undergoing major renovation works.
A staff member at the Beijing centre also directed the Post to the statement when asked specifically about the Fung Wah Estate report.
The safety document presented to residents by contractor Man Yuen Construction Engineering Company Limited indicated the scaffolding nets used in the project were up to code in terms of various safety standards, including fire safety.
Works at Fung Wah Estate came under scrutiny after the Tai Po fire, as the scaffolding nets used there were from the same Shandong manufacturer as Wang Fuk Court.

A copy of the report, apparently conducted by the “National Quality Supervision and Inspection Center for Labour Protective Equipment (Beijing)”, indicated the tests were done on behalf of the manufacturer in Shandong in January this year, and included signatures and official stamps.
The report indicated “No. Issuance and Quality Inspection (Net) 2025-023” in Chinese on its cover, suggesting it was a serial number.
“As verified by our centre, we have never issued a testing report with the numbering ‘No. Issuance and Quality Inspection (Net)’ prefix since October 2019,” the centre’s statement added.
“Reports issued by our center all have strict anti-counterfeiting features … the public can verify the reports by scanning the QR code on the report or by contacting our centre.”
The statement concluded by condemning the forgery created in the centre’s name, as it “severely disrupts market order and poses a potential threat to public safety”.
“We reserve the right to pursue legal accountability against the relevant parties,” the centre said.
Responding to queries from the Post, a staff member of Man Yuen Construction Engineering Company said the report was provided by the scaffolding subcontractor and that the firm was also looking into the matter.
She added the company had no further comment and would only speak to official government investigators.

data.title: Can Seoul count on China’s commitment to Korean reunification?
data.description:
China’s role in any future reunification of the Korean peninsula should not be assumed, a South Korean analyst has warned at a peace forum in Hong Kong after being asked if Bejing is a reliable partner to help advance the goal.
Sung Ki-young, chief research fellow at the Institute for National Security Strategy, told the Korean Peninsula Peace Forum on Wednesday that both China and the United States were reluctant to rapidly or radically alter peace and stability on the peninsula, meaning major powers were more likely to support incremental steps than any sudden shift.
He said international players preferred “a more step-by-step approach” towards peace and the long-stalled prospect of reunification between the two Koreas.
In that context, Sung said China was “not 100 per cent reliable” when it came to its role in reunification, given Beijing’s preference for stability as a constraint on how far it would be willing to go.
Sung argued, however, that Beijing’s influence on North Korea remained unmatched because China still provided what he called a “life support function for North Korea’s fragile economy”.
“If China locks the pipeline, North Korea will not be able to survive,” said Sung, who previously served as director general for unification policy at South Korea’s Ministry of Reunification.


China remains North Korea’s principal economic partner and is widely seen as providing a major share of its trade and essential supplies such as food and fuel, as well as access to foreign currency, including through cross-border channels that have persisted despite international sanctions.
Sung said South Korea still saw Beijing as indispensable in efforts to reduce tensions and bring North Korea back to talks on its nuclear programme.
“Whether reliable or not, the South Korean government sees China as the most important player in persuading or dissuading [North Korea’s] denuclearisation,” he said.
China has, at times, shown it can apply meaningful pressure to Pyongyang. In 2017, it backed tough UN sanctions and enforced measures such as cutting refined oil exports to North Korea and banning imports of North Korean textiles. But Beijing has generally preferred to act as a facilitator rather than an enforcer, consistently urging dialogue as the path to denuclearisation.
And while Beijing has publicly supported the “peaceful reunification” of the Korean peninsula through dialogue and negotiation, it has also stressed that “upholding peace and stability on the Korean peninsula and advancing the political settlement process … serves the common interests of all parties”, according to a 2024 statement from China’s foreign ministry.

Such ambiguity in Beijing’s position has become a key factor in Seoul’s foreign policy debates.
Sung said South Korea’s relations with China under former president Yoon Suk-yeol had been “bad” and “too ideological”, and Seoul would now have to deliver to Beijing ways to “influence North Korea to come back to diplomacy”.
Chinese President Xi Jinping and his South Korean counterpart Lee Jae-myung met last month at the Apec summit held in Gyeongju, and Lee is seeking to visit China as early as January for a meeting with Xi, according to recent South Korean media reports.
Kim Ji-young, professor at the Institute for Unification Education at the Ministry of Unification, told the forum the Lee administration had adopted the strategy of “pragmatic realism” and “active peace” when it came to North Korea.
This, he said, was a departure from the “rigid approaches” of the previous administration by prioritising the safety and lives of the people over ideological confrontation.
“The Ministry of Unification assesses that the current stand-off on the Korean peninsula requires agile crisis management rather than rigid ideological posturing,” Kim said.
Ideological confrontation between the two Koreas intensified due to the Yoon administration’s “value-oriented” foreign policy – which prioritised universal values such as freedom, liberal democracy and the rule of law – and North Korea’s reciprocal declaration of its southern neighbour as a hostile state.
This approach marked a sharp departure from the policy of engagement under Yoon’s predecessor Moon Jae-in.

On Wednesday, Lee said he remained hopeful that US leader Donald Trump could persuade North Korea to resume dialogue, noting that Pyongyang appeared to take Washington more seriously than Seoul in sustaining its regime.
At a press briefing to mark the anniversary of the martial law crisis with foreign correspondents at Cheong Wa Dae in Seoul, Lee said he intended to extend an apology to North Korea over the previous government’s alleged dispatch of drones to Pyongyang to distribute anti-regime leaflets.
The Korean Peninsula Peace Forum was organised by the Consulate General of the Republic of Korea in Hong Kong and The Peaceful Unification Advisory Council, a presidential consultative body aimed at establishing and implementing policies on democratic and peaceful unification.

data.title: Fireworks shop owner dies from pesticide after reporting officials: Chinese media
data.description:
A fireworks shop owner in Hunan province died from drinking pesticide after publicly reporting officials for disciplinary violations, Chinese media reported.
The owner, surnamed Peng, had recently published a video of himself drinking the substance, local authorities in Chenzhou, Hunan province, said in a notice on Monday.
A local task force was investigating the matter to ascertain if the allegations were true and would “deal with the incident according to laws and regulations without mercy”, the statement said.
Peng died on Tuesday, according to Jiupai News, a Hubei-based official media outlet, which quoted a friend of Peng’s.
A person affiliated with Chenzhou No 1 People’s Hospital told Jiupai News that Peng had died after being taken there for treatment, saying “he had drunk too much poison” and that “it was way over a lethal amount”.
Separately, the Shandong-based news outlet Qilu Evening News on Monday reported that the shop exploded on Sunday.

A video circulating online showed flames and smoke at the shop, accompanied by a huge blast, with the shop’s walls collapsing in and a white car parked by the roadside overturned.
On Wednesday, the mainland magazine China Newsweek quoted Peng’s widow as saying Peng had recently been ordered by “multiple departments” to close his shop on the grounds of “failing to meet air quality standards”.
Peng had more than 2 million yuan (US$283,000) in outstanding loans for the stock amassed at his shop and had asked officials if he could keep operating his business, but they refused him, according to the magazine, which is affiliated with the official China News Service.
The report cited Peng’s widow – without stating her name – as saying Peng learned officials would forcibly close the shop on Sunday, prompting Peng to detonate explosives at 7am that morning and destroy the three-year-old business.
It added that a complaint letter, written by Peng and provided by his widow, revealed he had previously complained about “lazy governance” by officials from the city’s environmental authority.
Multiple media outlets have called for a thorough investigation into what happened.
In a commentary on Wednesday, The Beijing News described the incident as “complex and far-reaching” and a combination of “a workplace safety accident, a real-name report against public officials, extreme individual behaviour and possible policy implementation disputes”.
The newspaper urged local authorities to “swiftly ascertain all facts, handle the matter in accordance with laws and regulations, bring solace to the deceased and provide the public with a satisfactory explanation”.
China in recent years has seen multiple instances of citizens publicly reporting misdeeds, including corruption and sexual misconduct, using their real names on social media and attracting nationwide attention.
Last year, following a corruption investigation into a female township official in Jiangxi province, the official’s father filed a real-name report accusing Mao Qi, the top official and Communist Party secretary of Wannian county in Jiangxi, of sexually assaulting her.
The incident gained nationwide attention and led to further investigations by provincial authorities. In April, Mao was sentenced to 10 years and six months in prison for bribery.
In another high-profile case last year, a doctoral student at Renmin University in Beijing posted a nearly hour-long video on social media, including audio evidence, accusing her supervisor of sexual assault.
Wang Guiyuan, a literature professor, was subsequently dismissed from his post and expelled from the party.
